learning_outcomes.description: Review and analyse the principles and concepts that underpin the planning profession and planning practice.
Breakdown and address complex planning issues
Navigate and review a planning scheme and develop planning solutions to a complex development theme.
Demonstrate an appreciation of the ethics and ethical standards of the planning profession.
Summarise and explain the role of planners working independently and in multi-disciplinary teams.
Prepare a professional planning report for local development project.

assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Notebook/ Logbook, Participation/ Student contribution, Practical/ Demonstration, Reflection
assessments.task_description:
Assessment Rationale

This assessment requires students to complete and submit an individual workbook based on activities undertaken during Weeks 2–6. As this is a technical course, regular attendance and active participation are essential for successful completion of the assessment tasks.

Students are expected to:

  • Attend and participate in workshops with policy specialists, followed by a one-hour practical session in Weeks 2–5.
  • Attend the Development Intensive (Brisbane City field trip) in Week 6. Students who are unable to attend the field trip may be required to complete alternative activities, subject to approval by the Course Coordinator.
  • Complete the required independent research and readings.

The workbook will be made available in Week 2 and can be accessed via Blackboard.

Complex / authentic assessment using AI and/or MT to support learning: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The School of Architecture, Design and Planning uses Blackboard and Turnitin for assessment submission. Turnitin is accessed through the course Blackboard site. Turnitin also checks for plagiarism or instances where the original work of others is not appropriately acknowledged. Uploaded files must contain readable text and not be rasterised. Uploaded files must contain readable text and not be rasterised. Students are advised to commence assignment uploads with sufficient amount of time (consider possible technical problems with computers, internet speed, etc). After successfully submitting an assignment through Turnitin, a ‘Submission Complete!’ screen will be displayed. It is the student’s responsibility to check assignment preview and confirm successful submission. If the ‘Submission Complete!’ screen isn’t displayed, the student should regard the submission as unsuccessful. Students should download a copy of the digital receipt as proof they have submitted the assignment. Students who are experiencing upload issues must advise the Course Coordinator immediately by email and should include screenshots and a copy of the assessment for submission. To meet professional accreditation, public engagement and quality assurance obligations, digital copies of all course assessment items must be submitted in addition to any hard copy submission requirements specified in individual Course / Studio Outlines. Any physical models should be photographed and a minimum of two photographs describing the complete and full model must be included in the digital submission.

assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Notebook/ Logbook, Participation/ Student contribution, Practical/ Demonstration, Presentation, Reflection
assessments.task_description:
Assessment Rationale

Skills Module Two focuses on the application of statutory planning concepts, legislation, and planning tools. Students are required to critically analyse development proposals within the Queensland planning system and demonstrate their understanding through the completion of workbook activities, culminating in a professional presentation and question-and-answer session with the class.

This assessment involves:

  • Attendance and active participation in a series of weekly workshops with a statutory planning specialist. Through these sessions, students will develop foundational knowledge and practical skills in statutory planning and development assessment. Student attendance at these sessions will be tracked.
  • Completion and submission of an individual workbook that documents and reflects on activities undertaken during the workshops (Weeks 6–10).
  • A professional presentation in Week 11, in which students will analyse the statutory planning issues associated with an assigned development scenario. Presentations will be five minutes in length and supported by presentation slides.
  • Participation in a question-and-answer session, where students will be required to explain and defend their analysis.

Completion of Skills Module Two includes three components: Presentation Slides (25%), Presentation (25%) and Workbook (50%). These individual components are submitted and completed over three days in Week 11.

Guidance on the presentation structure, analytical framework, and graphic requirements will be provided via Blackboard.

The workbook will be made available in Week 7 and can be accessed through Blackboard.

Complex / authentic assessment using AI and/or MT to support learning: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

assessments.due_date:
Planning Report and Reflection 13/11/2026 2:00 pm

Digital Submission: via Blackboard.
assessments.task_description:
Assessment Rationale

This is an individual assessment in which you will prepare a Planning Report for an innovative development proposal. Due to the project's design, location, and potential impacts, the proposal is considered impact assessable and requires development approval under the Queensland planning system.

The project should demonstrate leading practice in addressing one or more strategic planning priorities, including (but not limited to):

  • Affordable housing
  • Climate-responsive design
  • Liveability

Students must select a development proposal and site from the list provided on Blackboard. A report template will also be provided for students to follow.

This assessment is in two parts:

  1. Planning Report (90%) - A professional planning report that critically assesses and justifies the proposed development against the Queensland Planning System. The report must not exceed 5,000 words, including quotations. The word limit does not include appendices, tables, or the application form completed using the relevant Queensland Government templates.
  2. Practice Reflection (10%) - An individual reflection that demonstrates your learning and professional development throughout the project. The reflection must follow the structure and requirements provided on Blackboard.

Further assessment details, including submission requirements and supporting resources, are available on Blackboard.

Complex / authentic assessment using AI and/or MT to support learning: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
